speaker: interviewee
text: Before we conclude-- this is already a conclusion-- I want to make a correction about our daughter, Pamela. Pamela was a student at the University of Michigan, as I told you before. And it was my fault, my misunderstanding. And I should have paid more attention to-- like I said before-- to raising our children. Well, Pamela rebelled. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] She wanted to quit, you know, to try to make up her mind what she is going to do, as far as continuing education. And she wanted to drop out of school for awhile and then go back. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] On a conference with wife, Florence, we decided that Pamela should finish school and then drop out. And if she wished to go back again for advanced degree or some other field, she may do so. But she had to finish school first. Because we saw what's going on in society, that once they drop out of school it's a difficult-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we find that people have difficulty going back to school and picking up where they left off. So I hope Pamela will forgive me and she'll understand. Thank you. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And, Martin, who are these people in the photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: My pleasure. This my father and my mother, their engagement picture, which they sent to America to my father's sister, Bertha Frumin. That's how it happened to survive. No other pictures are available of them when they were young.

speaker: interviewer
text: Martin, where was this picture taken and who are the people in it?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a very cute picture. Starting from left to right, this is my Uncle Hillel, whose last name I don't remember. But I know it's my Uncle Hillel. This was taken in Rovno before the war. Next to Uncle Hillel, the little girl is my cousin, Chaika Kobylanska, who perished, was brutally murdered in the village of Glinsk, G-L-I-N-S-K, about five miles from Giuszwica. The lady is my Grandma Kobylanska, who perished with Chaika's little sister, whose name I don't remember. Next to my grandma is my brother, Robert. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And next to him, the last one on the right, that's me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yes, sir.

speaker: interviewer
text: Martin, where is this picture taken and who are the people in it?

speaker: interviewee
text: This wonderful picture was taken in Detroit, Michigan, June 7, 1947, at the Michigan Central Depot. When we got out of the train from New York somebody did us a nice favor and took us this picture. And I have a suspicion it was my cousin, Mo. From left to right, Eva Frumin, sister-in-law to my aunt, Bertha Frumin. Next to her is our good uncle, uncle Max Frumin, husband to Bertha Frumin. Next to him is uncle Jack Kobylanska, brother of my father who was in the Soviet Union during the war building factories in the Euros. Next to him is his sister, Bertha Frumin, our good charm lady. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Next to her is my mother, Rose Kobylanska. And in front of her is my brother, Robert, whose Hebrew name is Yitzhak. Behind my mother is a gentleman, the father of Eva Frumin was far on the left. I don't remember his name, but my father knew him in the old country before the war, I think around World War I yet. I want to mention my father was born in 1898. So he was old enough to know him before World War I. And next to my father, that's me. I already had a guitar. And I brought it with me. I bought it from a Russian soldier for a liter of vodka. And as you notice, that's all the luggage we brought with us. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We thank greatly to our aunt and uncle for being so generous to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Martin, where was this picture taken and who are the people in it?

speaker: interviewee
text: This photograph was taken in the big Jewish cemetery in the city of Rovno, the northeast corner of the city. This was taken in 1944, early spring or maybe still winter, maybe February or March. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] From left to right, my uncle Chaim Kobylanski; his sister, Chaika Kromirs; and my father, Anshel Kobylanski. They went to the Jewish cemetery to visit the grave of our grandfather, my grandfather, their father, Fischel Kobylanski, who has-- my grandson has his name, has a middle name Fischel, whom I never met. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But I understand he was a fine man. You notice there are no gravestones at this cemetery. They were all taken by the people of the area and by the Germans to build the runway at the airfield in Rovno. That's what's left. Not a very pretty sight.

speaker: interviewer
text: Martin, where was this photograph taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This photograph was taken in the village of Giuszwica. This is my father's house. This was my parents' house. This was our house. And I want you to know this was a substantial house compared to the other houses in the village. It's a little changed. But it's still the same. This photograph was taken in 1991.

speaker: interviewer
text: Martin, where was this photograph taken and who was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: This photograph was taken in Czechoslovakia. This is Mr. Scastny, Mr. Vladimir Scastny, one of the righteous men. He's wearing a soldier's uniform of the Czech Liberation Army, of Czechoslovakia Liberation Army, which was organized in the Soviet Union, compliments of the Soviet Union. But he was never allowed to return to Giuszwica once he was demobilized to Czechoslovakia. He was the man that kept us so we could stay alive. So we stayed alive. The man who dug the pits and the pig stable, who put the floor up, the man who worked so, so hard in order to find us a place of hiding, a very nice man. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This photograph was taken somewheres in the early '50s, or maybe the late, late '40s. This was sent to my parents by Mr. Scastny from Czechoslovakia when it was still possible to receive correspondence from there. From left to right seated is Mrs. Scastny and Mr. Vladimir Scastny, our good friend. And behind them, from left to right, their older daughter, [INAUDIBLE]. And behind Mr. Scastny, the lady with the corsage, that's [INAUDIBLE] Scastny, their daughters. They have a son, but I do not have a photo of him. This photograph was sent to our father by Mr. Scastny when [INAUDIBLE] was engaged to be married. And I've kept it a long time, ever since. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Very nice people. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This photograph is a very nice photograph also, very significant to me. This photograph was taken somewhere in the late '80s, I don't know exactly when. In the center of the picture is Mr. Sergei Denishchuk, the son of Stakh Denishchuk. The two of them, Stakh, his father, and Sergei were our keepers, protectors, and providers. We were sitting in the pigsty in the attic. This time was in the attic. And his wife, to the right of him, and his three sons. The son on the left, next to the mother, the tallest one, Mykola, with the photo-- with the photo was here in my house as a guest. And I'll show you another shot of him. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This photograph, it's dated 1991. Mykola is in the squatting position and next, standing with him, is his wife, Maria. They are the people-- Mykola and Maria were here and our guests in our house from August through November of 1991. I invited their father, Sergei, to visit, but instead they came because the father couldn't make it, because of poor health. Mykola, Maria, and their daughter, [INAUDIBLE], were guests in my house and the guests of my brother also. They took the picture. This marks the grave and the forest on the way to Dzlatkowicze, where my relatives, my aunts, my cousins, my grandma, and the other Jewish people from the village were shot, killed, and buried. That's the only thing that exists, just dirt. But Mykola knew where it was. And Mykola was born in 1943. So then other people know where that grave is. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This photograph is another photograph of Mykola Denishchuk, his wife, and his daughter. From left to right, my brother, Robert, his wife, Linda. Behind Linda, the tall man is Mykola Denishchuk, the grandson of Stakh and the son of Sergei. His daughter in the white shirt is his daughter [INAUDIBLE]. Behind her, in the dark sweater with the white stripes, is her mother and wife of Mykola, Maria Denishchuk, who were present in our house and stayed in our house with us. My wife, Florence, and me. [LAUGHS] This is-- I don't know-- a very nice picture. From left to right, my cousin Yitzhak Steinboim of Bat Yam. Israel. Next to him is Dvora Adler. Now her married name is Mendelet. Haifa, Israel. Myself. My cousin, Pinchas Steinboim, brother of Yitzhak. Really present the younger generation of survivors from our village. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Dvora and her brother, Joe, are the only survivors of their family of people of nine. Pinchas is on the right and Yitzhak on the left, the brothers. And they are survivors. They were hidden out on a farm, on a Ukrainian farm not far from our village. They both survived. And they live in Israel. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This photograph was taken 25 years ago in Brooklyn, New York. Fascinating photo. From right to left, Joseph Korn-Adler, brother of Dvora that you saw in the previous photograph. His wife, Claire. Next to Claire, in the white jacket, is Isaac Ooster and his new bride when they got married. Next to her is my aunt, Malka, a survivor. I forgot to mention Joseph is a survivor from Giuszwica. Isaac Ooster, the groom, survived in Giuszwica. Malka, my aunt Malka, survived in Giuszwica. My mother, the lady with the glasses, my mother, Rose Kobylanski, naturally she survived in Giuszwica. Next to her is my aunt Chana, my mother's sister, three sisters in a row, also survived with a son, Rachmiel, who lives in Brooklyn. Next to her is her husband who died in New York-- in Israel. [INAUDIBLE]. Chana remarried. Next to [INAUDIBLE] is my beloved Florence, who was born in Detroit. And the next one to Florence is me, also survivor of Giuszwica. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] My beautiful family. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I only wish my father could see them. From left to right, my niece, our niece, Amy Koby; her mother, Linda Koby; my brother, Robert Koby; and myself. Next to me, to the right, is our daughter, Glenda. Next to her is her husband, our son-in-law, Bruce Spiegel. Next to Bruce is our son, Miles Koby. Next to him is a son, Daniel Whitney. From left to right, seated, is our daughter, Eileen Koby; my wife, Florence; our daughter, Pamela; our lovely daughter-in-law, Betty Koby; and our first grandson, Jay Koby. It's my most immediate family. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I want to introduce to you, to my right is my brother, Robert, with whom I spent all the hiding days together. It was not fun being hidden, but we survived. The two of us, we were all the time together in hiding, with our parents, and with aunt Chaika. To the left is my sister-in-law and the wife of my brother, Linda Koby.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do either of you have anything you'd like to say to Martin?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't prepared. But, like Marty said, it was an experience [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that's, I guess, not going to be forgotten until we pass. I'm glad to be in this country.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is part of my family, the bigger part of my family. Next to me is now my beloved Florence, who is the mother of my children, of our children. Behind us, from left to right, Pamela and Eileen and daughter, Glenda, who came from Washington DC, from Bethesda, Maryland, to visit us. Her husband and our son-in-law, nice young man, whom I love very much, Bruce Spiegel. For a man who was born in Poland, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] who has lovely children, graduates of universities in the United States, I'm very proud of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Florence, would you like to say something?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I'm grateful that my husband and his brother survived and their family survived. I wish more people had survived. I wish it had never happened. And we hope that this sort of thing never happens again. We're all brothers no matter what. And we just hope for peace and goodwill toward all men, all mankind.
